g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about Relational Model Constraints - the guardians of data integrity in databases.
Constraints are rules that restrict what data values can be stored, ensuring database integrity and accuracy.
g.text: Rules that restrict what data values can be stored in a database.
They act as guardians of data integrity, ensuring only valid and

g.data-narration: There are three main integrity constraints: Domain, Entity, and Referential - each protecting data differently.
Domain constraints define permissible values for attributes - each attribute must contain only values from its defined domain.
g.text: Domain = Set of atomic values with same data type
Ensures attribute values are valid, atomic, and comparable

g.data-narration: A superkey is a set of attributes that uniquely identifies each tuple - no two tuples can share the same values.
g.text: Superkey: Set of attributes that uniquely identifies each tuple

g.data-narration: A candidate key is a minimal superkey - removing any attribute destroys uniqueness. One is chosen as primary key.
g.text: Primary key must be unique and NOT NULL

g.data-narration: Entity integrity ensures the primary key cannot be NULL - every tuple must have a unique, non-null identifier.
g.text: Primary key must NOT contain NULL values

g.data-narration: Referential integrity maintains consistency between tables - foreign keys must match primary keys or be NULL.
g.text: Foreign key must match primary key in referenced table OR be NULL

g.data-narration: Foreign keys create relationships between tables, preventing dangling references to non-existent records.
When deleting parent records, DBMS can RESTRICT, CASCADE, SET NULL, or SET DEFAULT to maintain integrity.
g.text: When deleting a parent tuple with children, DBMS can:
Reject delete | Delete children | Set FK to NULL | Set FK to default

g.data-narration: A database schema defines the logical structure - tables, attributes, keys, and constraints of the entire database.
g.text: The logical structure of the entire database
Schema includes: relations, attributes, PKs, FKs, domains

g.data-narration: Schema is static structure (design), while instance is dynamic data - schema defines, instance contains actual values.
SQL enforces constraints using PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, and CHECK keywords automatically.
g.text: PRIMARY KEY | FOREIGN KEY | NOT NULL | UNIQUE | CHECK
DBMS automatically enforces all constraints at all times

g.data-narration: Remember: Entity (PK not NULL), Referential (FK valid), Domain (valid values) - constraints protect your data.
